coredata.dc:title: Green and efficient synthesis of carbon quantum dots from cordia myxa L. and their application in photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Najjar, Morvarid
Nasseri, Mohammad Ali
Allahresani, Ali
Darroudi, Majid
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized through a simple and green hydrothermal method with the usage of green materials. The synthesis of CQDs was confirmed by the results of various analyses such as XRD, EDX, FT-IR, and UV–Vis, while their size and morphology were determined by the TEM and FESEM images. This product contained a pseudo spherical morphology with an average size of 2.2 nm. The good photoluminescence (PL) intensity and quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs were confirmed using the PL spectra. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of the prepared CQDs was examined in the degradation of the four organic dyes including methylene blue (MB), crystal violet (CV), methyl orange (MO), and eriochrome black T (EBT) under UV irradiation, while the effective parameters including pH, amount of photocatalyst, and dye concentration were optimized.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Cordia Myxa L
Hydrothermal method
Photocatalyst
Organic dye

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from cordia myxa L

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the University of Birjand and Mashhad University of Medical Sciences for the technical and financial support of this work based on the Ph. D thesis of Ms. M. Najjar.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the preparation of the CQDs from Cordia myxa L.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) EDX spectrum, (c) Zeta potential analysis, and (d) FT-IR spectra of the synthesized CQDs.
(a) FESEM image, (b) TEM image, (c) particle size distribution histogram from TEM, (d) two-dimensional, and (e) three-dimensional AFM images of the synthesized CQDs.
(a) UV–Vis spectrum and (b) the bandgap energy of the CQDs.
Excitation depended on emission spectra of the CQDs aqueous solution.
Degradation of MB, EBT, CV, and MO dyes in the presence of the CQDs under UV irradiation.
(a) The effects of EBT dye concentration, (b) pH, and (c) the CQDs concentration in the dye degradation.
(a) Decomposition of EBT in the presence of the CQDs under UV irradiation and (b) the pseudo-first-order kinetic curve.
Schematic of EBT photodegradation by CQD under UV irradiation.
Trapping examinations of active species during photocatalytic degradation of EBT.
The comparison of synthesized CQDs with other different reported CQDs used for photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes in water.

head.ce:title: Green and efficient synthesis of carbon quantum dots from cordia myxa L. and their application in photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Birjand, P. O. Box 97175-615, Birjand, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Birjand, P. O. Box 97175-615, Birjand, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Medical Biotechnology and Nanotechnology, School of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Medical Sciences, Neyshabur University of Medical Sciences, Neyshabur, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment of Basic Medical Sciences, Neyshabour University of Medical Sciences, Neyshabour, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author: Majid Darroudi, Modern Sciences and Technologies, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Azadi Square, Mashhad 9177948564, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized through a simple and green hydrothermal method with the usage of green materials. The synthesis of CQDs was confirmed by the results of various analyses such as XRD, EDX, FT-IR, and UV–Vis, while their size and morphology were determined by the TEM and FESEM images. This product contained a pseudo spherical morphology with an average size of 2.2 nm. The good photoluminescence (PL) intensity and quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs were confirmed using the PL spectra. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of the prepared CQDs was examined in the degradation of the four organic dyes including methylene blue (MB), crystal violet (CV), methyl orange (MO), and eriochrome black T (EBT) under UV irradiation, while the effective parameters including pH, amount of photocatalyst, and dye concentration were optimized.

ce:section.ce:para: Throughout the past decade, carbon was recognized as a black and water-insoluble substance with low fluorescence. Due to the advent of nanoscience, many advances occurred in the development of science and technology such as the completely different properties of carbon nanostructures induced by their macrostructures. The CQDs are 0D carbon nanomaterials with very small sizes (1-10 nm) along with an amazing fluorescence [1]. These particles were accidentally discovered by Xu et al in 2004 [2] and labeled as ‘CQDs’ by Sun et al. in 2006 [3]. The structure of CQD includes carbon atoms with SP2 hybridization, as well as functional groups on its surface including hydroxyl, carboxylate, and amines [4]. These functional groups increase the factor of solubility in water and facilitate the conditions for binding to other reactive chemical groups for surface inactivation and being applied for the modification of organic, inorganic, polymeric, or biological materials [5,6].

The CQDs can be synthesized through “bottom-up” and “top-down” approaches [7,8]. Top-down approaches are referred to the physical exfoliation of large structures into quantum-sized particles of carbon with clean surfaces and high crystallinity features, which includes electrochemical etching [9] and laser ablation [10]. On the other hand, bottom-up approaches involve a carbonization process of small molecules or polymers with low molecular weight such as microwave-assisted synthesis [11], hydrothermal [12,13], solvothermal [14], and ultrasonication treatment [15], and chemical etching [16]. Among the varying synthesizing methods for CQDs, the effectiveness of the hydrothermal method was confirmed due to several unique features such as being efficient, green, and facile [17]. The unique properties of CQDs resulted in their widespread applications, which include chemical sensing [18], photocatalysis [19], electrocatalysis [20], bioimaging [21], drug delivery [22], solar cells [23], bio-labeling [24], and biomedicine [25].

In recent years, numerous reports were presented on the topic of natural biomass as a carbon resource for the synthesis of CQDs. For example, Hu et al. succeeded in synthesizing CDs through a hydrothermal method with the usage of coal and applied the product as a photocatalyst [26]. In addition, Tadesse et al. exerted lemons to synthesize CQDs for cellular imaging applications [27], while Aslandas et al. prepared fluorescent CQDs by the usage of Blueberry for facilitating the detection of Fe3+ ions [28]. The work of D'souza et al. presented data on obtaining CQDs from carrots for drug delivery purposes [29]. Among the many advantages of natural bio-waste and bio-resource, one can state the features of being low cost, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly [30]. In addition, the CQDs have superior properties over other semiconductor quantum dots such as water solubility, stable photoluminescence, easy modification, and chemical immobility, as well as good compatibility and stability against photobleaching [31].

Recently, studies on the removal of water pollution attracted the attention of many researchers since dye pollution has caused irreparable risks to the health of humans and the environment throughout the past years [32]. In the position of fluorescent nanomaterials, these CQDs can function as suitable photocatalysts for dyes degradation due to their unique optical properties such as significant optical sensors and adjustable emission profiles [33]. The Cordia myxa L. is a fruit with excessive medicinal and nutritional uses. Once this fruit becomes over-ripened and falls off its tree, it is considered a waste due to turning into dark purple and getting inedible. Therefore, it was investigated as a possible precursor to protecting the environment, as well as providing a low-cost and alternative source for CQD. For this purpose, our product was synthesized by the usage of Cordia myxa L. through green and simple hydrothermal method. Various analyzing procedures such as FT-IR, FESEM, EDX, XRD, TEM, UV–Vis, and PL were exerted to confirm the synthesis and size of obtained nanoparticles with the good QY. Different dyes were evaluated to investigate their photocatalytic activity throughout the degradation of water pollution in the presence of synthesized CQDs. According to the obtained results, these CQDs proved to be capable of exhibiting significant photocatalytic activity in the removal of organic dyes.

Fresh Cordia myxa L. was collected from different regions of North Khorasan province in Iran. The MB, EBT, MO, and CV dyes and quinine sulfate dihydrate (Biological Reagent) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co and Aladdin Biochemical Co. All of the involved materials were exerted in the analytical reagent grade, while distilled water was applied in the performed investigations. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Thermo Nicolet, AVATAR 370) was exerted to identify the existing functional groups and an X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was detected by the employment of an XRD diffractometer (X'pertpro Pro-MPD) with Cu Kα radiation. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were determined through an electron microscope (JCM) and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy with an acceleration voltage of 20 kV was acquired. The Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was achieved by the utilization of a TEM microscope (AB LEO912), while the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was determined by the means of a Brisk Multi-Mode atomic force microscope. Furthermore, the zeta potential measurements were obtained by the usage of a zeta potential analyzer. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was measured through a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (CE9500) and the PL spectrum was recorded using a spectrophotometer (FL4600).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from cordia myxa L
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method by involving the extraction of Cordia myxa L. as the carbon precursor (Fig. 1 ). Initially, 1.0 g Cordia myxa L. was mechanically grinded into a fine powder to be added to an amount of deionized water (100 mL) and stirred at 45 °C for 2 h. the obtained mixture was transferred to an autoclave and warmed up in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h. Then the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The obtained solution was centrifuged to remove the larger nanoparticles and impurities. Lastly, the dark brown solution was filtered via a 0.22 μm filter membrane for further purification and freeze-dried into powder.

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized CQDs was investigated by the means of a self-made reactor, which was equipped with a UV lamp (20 W) for excitation irradiation in the UV area. Initially, 0.3 mg of CQDs was added to prepared solutions of MB, MO, EBT, and CV dyes (1.5 × 10−5 M, 100 mL). Then, the mixtures were kept in a dark room to achieve an equilibrium in the absorption and desorption before the step of light irradiation. Thereafter, each solution was placed under a UV lamp at a distance of 10 cm and their absorbance was measured with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer in a range of 200 – 600 nm. The degradation percentage of dyes was calculated through the following Eq. (1) [34].
(1)
D e g r a d a t i o n r a t e
( % )
=
A 0
−
A t
A 0
× 100

A 0
=
Absorption of the initial solution

A t
=
Absorption of solution after t min

The QY of CQDs, which is generally dependent on the exerted synthesizing method, the nature of precursors, and the applied deactivation method was calculated through Eq. (2) [35,36].
(2)
Ф R
=
Ф S
.
(
G r a
d R
/ G r a
d S
)
.
(
η R 2
/
η S 2
)
in which, S and R subscripts stand for quinine sulfate as a standard and the CQDs as a sample, respectively.
Ф S
stands for the QY of fluorescence from quinine sulfate (
Ф S
: 0.54
) that is measured in H2SO4 (0.1 M). Grad displays the gradient from the plot of fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance, and η represents the refractive index of applied solvent, namely water for the CQDs and H2SO4 for quinine sulfate which was observed to be 1.3325 and 1.336, respectively [37]. To calculate the QY, it was initially required to prepare five concentrations of quinine sulfate in H2SO4 and the CQDs in water. Then the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of each solution were measured on
λ
e x
: 430 nm. The plot of fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance was drawn for the CQDs and quinine sulfate, which resulted in obtaining 54.89, 0.9947, and 14% for
G r a
d R
/ G r a
d S
,
η R 2
/
η S 2
. and QY of CQDs, respectively.

The XRD pattern of the CQDs was exhibited in Fig. 2 a. The broad peak was observed at around
2 θ :
20
∘
that was related to the (002) plane and this result corresponds well with what Liu et al. reported [38,39]. The result confirmed the amorphous nature of the synthesized CQDs and the carbon source in Cordia myxa L. had been effectively converted to CQDs.

The major chemical composition of prepared CQDs is displayed in Fig. 2b which was surveyed through the application of EDX analysis. According to the observations, the synthesized product is composed of three elements including carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) with weight ratios of 44.13, 45.73, and 10.14 %, respectively.

Zeta potential analysis was carried out (Fig. 2c) by using the dilute solution of CQDs that resulted in determining a value of -44.3 mV, which corroborated the existence of negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CQDs. Consequently, the existence of these hydrophilic groups on the CQD surface can lead to the high solubility of this product.

The polar functional groups existing on the CQDs surface were identified using the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2d). The broad peak at 3394 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the O-H band, as well as the adsorbed water on the CQDs surface [40]. Another peak can be observed at 2928 cm−1 indicating the stretching vibration of the C-H group [40]. The absorption peaks at 1711 and 1669 cm -1 were allocated to the stretching vibration of the C=O band, which is related to the carboxyl group and amid group, respectively [41,42]. The perceived peaks at 1599 and 1404 cm -1 can be associated with the stretching vibrations of the COOH group [43]. The N-H stretching vibration of amid group can be seen at 1518 cm -1 [44]. The observed peaks at 1202, 1073, and 1032 cm -1 were allocated to the stretching vibrations of the C-O band of the carboxyl group, C-N band of amid group, and C-O band of the hydroxyl group, respectively [45]. The stretching vibrations of the epoxide group have appeared at 811 and 771 cm -1 [46].

The morphology of CQDs was investigated by the FESEM analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the product is composed of very tiny particles that agglomerated and have led to larger chunk-like structures [19]. The agglomeration can have occurred for several reasons such as the small size of CQD, humidity, temperature, and residence time [47,48].

The TEM images and particle size analysis (PSA) analyzed the size and morphology of CQDs. Accordingly, next to their pseudo spherical shape, the results displayed the good dispersion of the CQDs throughout their environment (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3c demonstrates the size distribution curve of obtained CQDs from the TEM image, while the average size was shown to be 2.2 nm.

In addition to TEM images, the morphology of CQDs was examined through the AFM images [49]. The AFM topological image of CQDs is exhibited in Fig. 3d. The provided statistics from the AFM image indicated that the height distribution of CQDs was centered at ∼5.5 nm while the surface uniformity of the CQDs was confirmed with an average thickness of 1.5 nm (Fig. 3e).

The significant optical properties of CQDs were examined by the exertion of UV–Vis and PL spectroscopy in an aqueous solution. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs was illustrated in the range of 200 to 600 nm (Fig. 4 a). A weak absorption peak was shown at 223 nm in correspondence to the π→π⋆ transition of the C=C group, while the maximum absorption peak at 283 nm was attributed to the n→π⋆ transition of the C=O group on the surface of CQDs [50]. According to Fig. 4a, the aqueous solution of the CQDs has a transparent brown color under visible irradiation, while appearing in a clear green color under UV irradiation at 365 nm. As it is shown in Fig. 4b, the bandgap of this product was calculated by considering the extrusion of
(
α h ν
)
2
vs.
h ν
curve and determined to be at 4.07
e V
. Due to being greater the bandgap of CQDs than
3 e V
(
E g
≥ 3 e V
)
, electrons can be excited only through UV irradiation [51].

PL spectrum of the CQDs can provide data on the behavior of their excitation-dependent emission spectra, which is presented in Fig. 5 . The excitation of the CQDs at the wavelength range of 390 to 550 nm led to the emission of wavelengths from 500 to 560 nm. The strongest emission peak was observed at 430 nm excitation with an emission wavelength of 515 nm. The PL intensity decreased as the excitation wavelength increased from 430 to 550 nm. An understandable explanation for the behavior of PL can be due to the presence of CQDs with different sizes and surface energy traps. Furthermore, an enlargement in the size of CQDs can cause an increase in their excitation wavelength. The PL intensity at a specific excitation wavelength is dependent on the exciting particle number. The hugest number of particles were excited at the 430 nm excitation since the highest PL intensity from CQDs was recorded at this wavelength [52].

The photocatalytic activity of the CQDs throughout the degradation of organic dyes was executed by the presence of their surface functional groups. The absorbance of a photon is caused that electrons being excited from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) towards the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LOMO), which leads to the formation of oxidative holes and reduction of electrons in HOMO and LOMO, respectively. New electron fields or increasing bending bands on the surface of CQDs create different intensities in the presence of certain groups including O or N atoms. Moreover, COOH, OH, and NH groups were caused by an increase in hole-electron pairs and an accession in photocatalytic activity [6]. The photocatalytic activity of the CQDs was investigated in the degradation of four dyes, such as MB, MO, EBT, and CV, which were prepared in the initial concentration of (1.5 × 10−5 M) as the target pollutants (Fig. 6 ). The EBT dye was completely destroyed as its absorbance reached zero in the presence of CQDs after 45 min of UV irradiation. In addition, the decomposition of MB was performed before 60 min under UV irradiation. The other experimented dyes under UV irradiation involved MO and CV, which were both completely removed in 60 min. The degradation of dyes was carried out through a radical mechanism, referring to that certain parts were decomposed by creating reactive radical species under UV irradiation.

According to the mentioned results, the highest rate of dye degradation was assigned to EBT in the presence of the CQDs under UV irradiation. Therefore, EBT was selected as the optimal dye for investigating the influence of other parameters, such as dye concentration, pH, and dosage of photocatalyst, on the rate of dye degradation.

The effect of the initial concentration of dye was assessed in the presence of the synthesized CQDs on the degradation of EBT dye under UV light. For this purpose, the amount of the CQD (0.30 mg) was fixed while the EBT concentration (1.5 × 10−5 M, 100 mL) was doubled and tripled. The dye solutions were exposed to UV irradiation for 45 min to measure the absorption at regular intervals by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. In conformity to the outcomes, the CQDs can destroy more dye molecules due to their large surface area (Fig. 7 a). Additionally, it was found that with increasing EBT dose, a longer period was required for preparing a sufficient amount of reactive oxide species (ROS) to achieve complete dye degradation.

Considering the existence of charged functional groups on the surface of synthesized CQDs, it was necessary to study the effect of pH value on the degradation rate of the dye. For this purpose, the pH range of 4, 7, and 10 were applied to the dye solution with an initial concentration of EBT (1.5 × 10−5 M) in the presence of CQDs (0.30 mg) under UV irradiation. The resulting solution was placed under UV irradiation for 40 min, while its adsorption was measured at regular time intervals by the means of the spectrophotometer. Then, the percentage of dye degradation at different pH values was calculated by Eq. (1) and the results are presented in Fig. 7b. According to the diagram, the degradation percentage of EBT was enhanced by the induced increase in pH. Relatively, the highest percentage of photocatalytic degradation was assigned to the pH:10 with a result of 100% while the lowest percentage of photocatalytic activity belonged to the pH:4 with a degradation percentage of 68%. It is indicated by the outcomes that the pH of the reaction solution has a significant effect on the degradation rate due to the existing relationship between the pH value of the dye solution and the surface potential of the photocatalyst [53]. The influence of pH on the photocatalytic degradation of the dye can be attributed to hydroxyl radicals
(
O
H
•
)
produced by the holes in valence band
(
h
V B
+
)
that was obtained of the excitation of CQDs electrons. In addition, the hydroxyl radicals are produced of the oxidation of
O
H
−
and
H 2
O
(Eq. (3,4)):
(3)
O
H
−
+
h
V B
+
→ O
H
•
(4)
H 2
O +
h
V B
+
→ O
H
•
+
H
+

Since hydroxyl radicals are strong oxidizing radicals, they attack the azo group of the dye molecule to remove the dye from the solution. These radicals can also attack to aromatic structures of dye, but the degradation is achieved later than the decolorization of the dye [54]. In addition, the effect of pH on the photocatalytic process can be due to the following reasons: a) at low pH, H+ ions bond with the azo group and reduce the electrophilic property of this group; and b) pH affects dye adsorption and the photons adsorption [55,56]. In the base environment, the functional groups become more negative, which stands as a useful factor in electronic transitions throughout the degradation of EBT [53].

To optimize the amount of applied CQD, several solutions of dye with a constant initial concentration (1.5 × 10−5 M) at the optimal pH were prepared to be mixed with different amounts of the CQD. The resulting solutions were exposed to UV irradiation for 40 min after being exposed to darkness. Then, the absorption rate was measured throughout regular time intervals by the application of a spectrophotometer. The degradation percentage of dye in each solution was calculated by the usage of Eq. (1). According to the displayed data in Fig. 7c, increasing the amount of CQDs led to a decrease in the degradation percentage of EBT. The reason for this result is that the enhancing dose of CQDs was caused to increase the accumulation of the CQDs, which ultimately reduced the percentage of dye degradation.

The photocatalytic degradation of EBT dye was studied in the presence of CQDs in optimal conditions under UV irradiation (Fig. 8 a). The absorption band of EBT dye
(
λ max
= 570
nm
)
was related to the π→π* transition and exhibited the concentration of EBT in the aqueous solution. A gradual decrease in the peak intensity was indicative of the photocatalytic degradation of EBT, which was thoroughly investigated in the following (Fig. 8b). According to the results, the concentration vs. time changes is based on the pseudo-first-order kinetic model in conformity to Eq. (5).
(5)
ln
C 0
C t
= k t
k
is the degradation rate constant and
C 0
and
C
t
represent the initial concentration and concentration at time t. The value of the correlation coefficient
(
R
2
)
is shown in Fig. 8. Since
R
2
express the proportionality of the obtained data and model, attaining a value of 0.99 demonstrates that the model is perfectly appropriate.

Photocatalytic activity is dependent on various factors including absorption irradiation, electron transfer, band position, and operating efficiency of photogenerated carriers [52]. The electrons from the valance band (VB) are excited to the conduction band (CB) under UV irradiation. Therefore, the excellent functionality of excited CQDs as electron donors and acceptors can be confirmed. The excited electrons convert the adsorbed
O 2
on the CQDs surface to the superoxide ion-radical
(
O 2
• −
)
and
H 2
O 2
, while the produced holes convert
H 2
O
and
O
H
−
to
O
H
•
[57]. The
O
H
•
radicals are strong oxidants for the degradation of EBT dye. Reactive oxygen specie
O 2
• −
and the
h
V B
+
were created by the decolorization of EBT and resulted in the formation of water and carbon dioxide as their products [58]. The involved reactions are exhausted in Eq. (3,4, and 6-12) [59]. A schematic representation of the photocatalysis process was provided in Fig. 9 .
(6)
C Q D + h ν → C Q
D
*
(
h
V B
+
+
e
C B
−
)
(7)
O 2
+
e
C B
−
→
O 2
• −
(8)
2
H
+
+ 2
e
C B
−
+
O 2
→
H 2
O 2
(9)
H 2
O 2
+
O 2
• −
→ O
H
•
+
O 2
+ O
H
−
(10)
E B T + O
H
•
→ D e g r a d a t i o n
(11)
E B T +
O 2 •
→
H 2
O + C
O 2
(12)
E B T +
h
V B
+
→
H 2
O + C
O 2

Photocatalytic degradation of EBT dye in the presence of synthesized CQD was investigated through the trapping procedure of active species that involved the addition of different suitable scavengers. Thereafter, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) was used as the
O
H
•
scavenger, while benzophenone (BP) and sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) functioned as the
O 2
− •
scavenger and
h
+
scavenger, respectively [60]. In conformity to Fig. 10 , the addition of NaHCO3 causes a negative effect on the degradation process of EBT up to 77 %, which reduces the initial degradation rate of combining the inhibition, while inducing the degradation of
h
+
as well. After the addition of BP, the degradation rate of EBT faced a decrease down to 79%, which indicates the active role of
O 2
− •
throughout the removal of dye under UV irradiation [61,62]. However, IPA can significantly affect the degradation rate of EBT (42%) and prevent the production of
O
H
•
during the course of photocatalytic degradation. The reactivity arrangement of the initial radicals was set as the following:
O
H
•
≫
H 2
O
•
>
O 2
− •
[63,64]. Meanwhile,
O
H
•
is usually considered as the main reactant throughout the dye degradation.

To show the high performance, uniqueness, and other advantages of synthesized CQDs, we performed a comparison between our product and the previously reported CQDs concerning the degradation of organic dyes in water. In conformity to the relevant results listed in Table. 1 , the CQDs were able to annihilate the experimented dye in the shortest possible time when being compared to the other CQDs.

In this study, a one-step synthesis of fluorescence CQDs was presented via the green hydrothermal method from Cordia myxa L. as a carbon source. The synthesized CQDs had an average size of 2.2 nm with an amorphous structure and good QY. The photocatalytic activity of the CQDs was investigated in organic dye degradation. The EBT dye was degraded in the shortest possible time and was optimized the effective conditions for dye degradation. Therefore, it can be concluded that the synthesized CQDs can be proposed as a candidate for the dye degradation from wastewaters.
For this type of study, the ethical approval was not.
Not applicable.

Morvarid Najjar: Investigation, Design and perform experiments, Software, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Mohammad Ali Nasseri: Supervision, Project administration, Validation, Data curation, Writing - review & Editing. Ali Allahresani: Data curation, Writing - review & Editing. Majid Darroudi: Supervision, Project administration, Validation, Data curation, Writing - review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the University of Birjand and Mashhad University of Medical Sciences for the technical and financial support of this work based on the Ph. D thesis of Ms. M. Najjar.
Not applicable.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current and future perspectives of carbon and graphene quantum dots: From synthesis to strategy for building optoelectronic and energy devices
Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Ghosh, K. Sarkar, P. Devi, K.-H. Kim, P. Kumar, Current and future perspectives of carbon and graphene quantum dots: From synthesis to strategy for building optoelectronic and energy devices, Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.135 (2021) 110391.

J. Mater. Sci. Technol.
